rationale: Title X is more informative by embedding a concrete outcome metric (78% one-year arrhythmia freedom) directly in the title, which immediately signals the study's key finding to readers. The head-to-head framing ('versus') is cleaner and more decisive than Y's 'Feasibility, Safety, and 12-Month Efficacy' triad, which is a formulaic opener common in lower-tier journals. X also specifies the patient count (117), enhancing specificity and transparency. Y's structure front-loads procedural boilerplate ('Feasibility, Safety') before arriving at the meaningful comparison, and its ending is awkward ('Comparison to Radiofrequency' without specifying ablation). Both titles adequately name the pentaspline PFA system and the redo-AF context, but X communicates the bottom-line result more immediately and compellingly.
loser_weakness: Title Y uses a generic 'Feasibility, Safety, and Efficacy' triplet that reads formulaically; it buries the comparative design in a long subtitle and omits any outcome metric, leaving readers without a signal of the study's key finding. The trailing phrase 'Comparison to Radiofrequency' is grammatically incomplete and less informative than the explicit 'versus' framing in X.
